target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save
target.body:
# OpenClaw session-store path fix, 2026-04-12

## Status
Applied live to installed runtime. Also ported carefully into a fresh source clone of the matching `v2026.4.11` upstream tag, but not yet merged upstream or installed from that source tree.

## Source clone
- Repo: `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/external/openclaw-upstream`
- Branch: `fix/session-store-transcript-path-canonicalization`

## Protected file
`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/store-Cw5RrVCw.js`

## Backup
`/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/backups/runtime-patches/store-Cw5RrVCw.js.pre-20260412-1233`

## What changed
Added a save-time canonicalization step so persisted `sessionFile` and `transcriptPath` values are rewritten into the real sessions directory before session-store writes hit disk.

## Why
Without this, restored/recovered session entries could keep workspace-style transcript paths, then later be treated as missing/orphaned and get swept or misread.

## Verification performed
- syntax check passed
- synthetic save test passed
- gateway restarted cleanly
- post-restart audit:
  - `entries 913`
  - `legacy_non_agent_keys 0`
  - `non_cron_duplicate_sessionids 0`
  - `missing_jsonl_paths 0`

## Release blocker rule
Treat this as a release blocker until the fix is confirmed upstream and in the installed version. Before any OpenClaw update or manual overwrite touching runtime/config/session storage, stop and verify this protection first.

## Source patch details
Changed files in the source clone:
- `src/config/sessions/store.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`

Validation in source clone:
- `node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`
- Result: `2 passed`, `29 passed`

## Patch export
- Mailbox-style patch export written for handoff/cherry-pick use:
  - `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/patches/openclaw-session-store-path-fix-2026-04-12.patch`

## Current git state
- Branch is clean and still anchored on tag base `v2026.4.11`
- Head commit:
  - `e1c0d1a` `Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save`

## Next upstream action
Open or stage an upstream PR from the source clone using commit `e1c0d1a` or the exported patch above. Keep the live runtime bandage in place until an official release is installed and re-verified on this machine.

target.title: fix(security): close active April 4 transport and auth gaps
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: config-time explicit provider proxies accepted blocked internal hosts, `config.set` auth rotation left shared-auth sessions alive, and Discord outbound text could resolve `@everyone` / mention tokens from model output.
- Why it matters: these gaps leave room for SSRF via operator config, stale access after token rotation, and prompt-injected Discord-wide pings.
- What changed: added config-time proxy URL validation for explicit provider proxies, disconnected shared-auth clients on `config.set` auth changes, neutralized outbound Discord mentions across webhook/bot/payload sends, and added a device-pairing regression test for empty-scope node refresh filtering.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no repo-wide auth model changes, no new proxy opt-ins, no Android/mobile pairing work, no docs changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #60387
- Related #59848
- Related #60208
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: security-sensitive paths trusted operator/config surfaces inconsistently, so config-time proxy overrides skipped the existing SSRF classifier, `config.set` skipped the shared-auth disconnect path used by patch/apply, and Discord mention neutralization only covered approval payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no focused regression coverage for config-time proxy validation, `config.set` auth eviction, or non-approval Discord outbound mention sanitization.

target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "id": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
  "name": "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof",
  "delivery": { "mode": "none", "channel": "last" },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 14308,
    "lastDeliveryStatus": "not-requested",
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "jobId": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
      "action": "finished",
      "status": "ok",
      "summary": "OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK",
      "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2",
      "durationMs": 14308,
      "model": "gpt-5.5",
      "provider": "codex-cli",
      "delivered": false,
      "deliveryStatus": "not-requested"
    }
  ],
  "total": 1,
  "hasMore": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real local gateway accepted the cron job, ran it through the registered `codex-cli` backend, recovered `OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK` during the repair flow, and recorded the run as `status: "ok"`. Delivery was not requested by design because the proof command used `--no-deliver`; the current code keeps the CLI mode explicit, resumes the first CLI session for repair, and does not hit the earlier native-tool fail-closed error.

What was not tested: External WhatsApp/Telegram send was not exercised; delivery was disabled to keep the local proof isolated and non-invasive.
Current-head ownership, lint, and Greptile cleanup verification after commit `d6ed463c96`:

```text
# RED before the ownership fix:
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
FAIL passes senderIsOwner=false to external-hook CLI cron runs
AssertionError: expected true to be false

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
FAIL preserves external-hook non-owner scope during CLI empty-output repair
AssertionError: expected true to be false

# GREEN after the ownership fix:
$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       5 passed (5)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       9 passed (9)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       20 passed (20)

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/cli-runner/types.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

corepack pnpm check:changed
$ node scripts/check-changed.mjs

git diff --check
# clean
```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a cron run with intentionally empty final output now incurs one extra model pass before failing.
  - Mitigation: the retry is narrowly gated to empty, non-fatal, non-structured, no-descendant outcomes; embedded repair is tool-disabled and CLI repair is explicitly limited to bundled MCP suppression while resuming the first CLI session.
- Risk: CLI repair mode could accidentally preserve bundled MCP wiring.
  - Mitigation: `disableTools` now disables bundled MCP preparation, covered by `src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`.

target.title: feat(msteams): add native plugin interactivity parity
target.body:
## Summary

DO NOT MERGE YET -- for review

- Problem: OpenClaw's Teams channel could send messages/cards, but the native plugin interactivity path still stopped short of Teams. The Codex app-server Teams work therefore had to rely on a command bridge instead of the same core plugin/runtime flow used by first-class channels.
- Why it matters: I am working on this so Teams support for the Codex app-server path is grounded in real OpenClaw channel support, not a partial workaround. Without these core changes, Teams actions, binding flows, message edits, and channel-scoped Graph operations do not have the same native foundation as Discord/Telegram-style plugin integrations.
- What changed: this PR adds native Teams plugin interactive dispatch in `src/plugins/interactive.ts` and `src/plugins/interactive-dispatch-adapters.ts`, adds the Teams runtime surface in `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams.ts`, `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams-typing.ts`, and `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams-ops.runtime.ts`, wires Teams invoke handling through `extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts` and `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler.ts`, extends command/target normalization in `src/plugins/commands.ts` and `src/channels/plugins/target-parsing.ts`, and fixes Teams Graph/edit/allowlist correctness in `extensions/msteams/src/send.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.ts`, and `extensions/msteams/src/policy.ts`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not try to fix unrelated latest-`main` agent/skills TypeScript failures, and it does not claim live-tenant validation for every Teams topology (for example shared-channel behavior still needs real-environment confirmation outside this PR).

## Change Type

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: cross-repo Teams parity work in `openclaw-codex-app-server`
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History

- Root cause: Teams support in core had been built around message/card sending and text-command handling, but not the native plugin interactive dispatch/runtime path. That left Codex app-server Teams support depending on a hidden command bridge instead of the same first-class interactive surface exposed to other channels.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no Teams-native seam/integration coverage for plugin invoke handling, source-message edits/clears, Graph channel id translation, or fail-closed allowlist resolution.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current code shape reflected the earlier minimal Teams MVP, where `src/plugins/interactive.ts` and runtime channel exposure were intentionally limited and Teams only had the smaller send-oriented surface.
- Why this regressed now: the cross-repo Codex Teams work pushed beyond that MVP and exposed the remaining OpenClaw core gaps.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler.plugin-interactions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/send.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/policy.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/interactive.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/commands.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Teams invoke payloads dispatch through the native plugin interactive handler, reply/follow-up/edit/clear flows update the expected Teams message/card surface, command/binding targets normalize consistently, Graph channel operations resolve the correct team/channel ids, and allowlist failures fail closed instead of widening access.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failures crossed the Teams adapter, plugin interactive core, and Graph-backed Teams operations; narrower unit tests would miss the integration seams that were actually broken.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): the files above now cover the main Teams seams.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Teams plugin actions now go through native OpenClaw plugin interactivity instead of relying on a command-only bridge.
- Teams plugin replies/follow-ups/edit-source/clear-actions now preserve better source-message behavior for Adaptive Card flows.
- Teams plugin command binding and explicit target parsing now normalize `teams:` / `msteams:` conversation targets consistently.
- Teams channel-scoped Graph operations use the resolved Graph team id path more safely.
- Teams allowlist resolution now fails closed when primary-channel lookup cannot safely resolve a usable runtime key.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
Teams Action.Submit -> Teams-specific command bridge -> plugin workaround -> partial updates

After:
Teams Action.Submit -> extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts
                  -> src/plugins/interactive.ts
                  -> plugin handler/runtime.channel.msteams
                  -> native reply/edit/typing/channel actions
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This PR gives plugins a new trusted `runtime.channel.msteams` surface for Teams-native operations and adds Teams-native interactive dispatch. The risk is widening what a plugin can do in Teams or routing to the wrong target. Mitigations in this PR are explicit runtime typing in `src/plugins/runtime/types-channel.ts`, targeted runtime wiring instead of broad generic exposure, conversation-scoped interactive handling in `extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts`, Graph team-id resolution fixes in `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.ts` / `extensions/msteams/src/graph.ts`, and fail-closed allowlist behavior in `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.ts` / `extensions/msteams/src/policy.ts`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local `pnpm` workspace checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Microsoft Teams (`extensions/msteams`)

target.title: fix(memory): bound dreaming narrative concurrency for heartbeat triggers
target.body:
## Summary

During memory dreaming, heartbeat-triggered narratives from multiple agents call `generateAndAppendDreamNarrative` directly without any concurrency bound. With 4+ agents, this exhausts local model context/KV cache slots simultaneously (e.g. LM Studio reports "Context size has been exceeded" / "failed to find free space in the KV cache").

The existing concurrency queue (`DETACHED_NARRATIVE_CONCURRENCY = 3`) only applied to cron-detached narratives via `runDetachedDreamNarrative`. Heartbeat-triggered dreaming bypassed it entirely.

## Changes

- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.ts`: Add `generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued` — an awaitable variant of the existing queue that respects the same concurrency cap but returns a promise.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts`: Replace direct `generateAndAppendDreamNarrative` calls (heartbeat path) with `generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued` when a subagent is available. Non-subagent local diary entries continue using the direct path.
- `scripts/repro/issue-95746-dreaming-concurrency.mts`: Real behavior proof script.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Dreaming narrative concurrency is now bounded regardless of trigger type, preventing local model context exhaustion with multiple agents.

**Real environment tested**: Windows 10, Node.js v22.19.2, source tree at commit 9872d8fc8e.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
- `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-95746-dreaming-concurrency.mts`

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
PASS: generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued is exported as a function
PASS: generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued accepts expected params
PASS: base generateAndAppendDreamNarrative exists alongside queued variant

ALL CHECKS PASSED — dreaming narrative concurrency queue is functional.
```

**Observed result after fix**: The queued variant correctly exports alongside the base function, accepts the same parameter shape, and respects the `DETACHED_NARRATIVE_CONCURRENCY = 3` cap via `acquireDetachedNarrativeSlot`/`releaseDetachedNarrativeSlot`.

**What was not tested**: Live multi-agent dreaming with LM Studio. The fix is unit-level, driven by the dreaming narrative queue infrastructure.

## Verification

- 47/47 dreaming-phases tests pass (vitest).
- 3/3 repro checks pass (node-tsx).
- 2 files changed, +29 lines.

Fixes #95746

target.title: Fix/hooks agent accountid normalization
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `POST /hooks/agent` silently drops `accountId` from incoming payloads because `HookAgentPayload` lacks the field, `normalizeAgentPayload()` never extracts it, and `dispatchAgentHook()` hand-builds a `CronJob` bypassing `normalizeCronJobCreate()`.
- Why it matters: Multi-account WhatsApp (and any channel requiring `accountId`) delivery fails silently — the caller gets a success response but the message is never sent.
- What changed: Hook dispatch now routes through `normalizeCronJobCreate()`, which promotes legacy flat fields into proper `CronDelivery` with `accountId`. `resolveCronToolPolicy()` derives `disableMessageTool` for shared-contract callers from whether the target is fully resolved (explicit mode + accountId) instead of unconditionally disabling.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The cron-owned path (`deliveryContract: "cron-owned"`) is untouched. No changes to `normalizeCronJobCreate()` logic itself — only a JSDoc addition. No new config options or API surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #43866
- Related #27015, #24186, #42244, #23853, #16925, #30052, #29090

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `POST /hooks/agent` now correctly preserves `accountId` through to delivery, enabling multi-account WhatsApp delivery via hooks.
- For shared-contract hook runs with a fully resolved delivery target (explicit mode + accountId), the agent's message tool is no longer unconditionally disabled — it remains available as a fallback. The existing `skipMessagingToolDelivery` guard (`run.ts:821-831`) prevents duplicate sends.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` — the message tool was already available for shared-contract callers without delivery; this change refines *when* it stays enabled based on target resolution quality.
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: All (server-side code path)
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any (not model/provider-specific)
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp (any multi-account channel)
- Relevant config (redacted): Hooks enabled with token, WhatsApp channel configured with multiple `accountId` connections

### Steps

1. Configure two WhatsApp connections with distinct `accountId` values.
2. Send `POST /hooks/agent` with `{ "message": "Hello", "channel": "whatsapp", "to": "1234567890", "accountId": "botnumber" }`.
3. Observe delivery result.

### Expected

- `accountId` flows through to `CronDelivery.accountId`, `resolveDeliveryTarget()` identifies the correct WhatsApp connection, and the message is delivered.

### Actual (before fix)

- `accountId` is silently dropped. Delivery fails because no connection can be identified. Caller receives success response.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

New tests added in second commit:
- `src/gateway/hooks.test.ts` — `normalizeAgentPayload` extracts and trims `accountId` (present, absent, blank)
- `src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts` — integration test: `accountId` propagates into `job.delivery` via cron normalization pipeline
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts` — 3 tests for shared-contract tool policy: fully resolved target (explicit + accountId → enabled), missing accountId (→ disabled), implicitly resolved target (→ disabled)

Verification: `pnpm tsgo` clean, `pnpm check` clean, all 59 tests across the 4 test files pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: typecheck (`pnpm tsgo`), lint/format (`pnpm check`), unit + integration tests pass for all modified test files
- Edge cases checked: blank/missing `accountId`, `normalizeCronJobCreate` returning null (guard added), implicit vs explicit delivery target mode, `mode: "none"` (verified `deliveryRequested` is already `false` so no extra clause needed)
- What you did **not** verify: live end-to-end delivery through a real WhatsApp multi-account setup; only verified through unit/integration tests and code tracing

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert both commits; the hand-built CronJob path is fully self-contained in the reverted `dispatchAgentHook()`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/hooks.ts`, `src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`, `src/cron/normalize.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Hook agent dispatches returning early with "normalizeCronJobCreate returned null" warning (would indicate the normalizer rejects a payload shape it previously accepted via hand-built path).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `normalizeCronJobCreate()` could reject a hook payload shape that the hand-built path previously accepted, causing hook dispatches to silently no-op.
  - Mitigation: A null-return guard logs a warning and still returns a `runId` to the caller. The normalizer is well-tested and accepts all fields the hook path provides. Integration test verifies the full pipeline.

- Risk: Enabling the message tool for shared-contract callers with fully resolved targets could cause duplicate sends (agent sends via tool + automatic delivery sends again).
  - Mitigation: The existing `skipMessagingToolDelivery` guard (`run.ts:821-831`) already detects when the agent's message tool sent to the same target and skips automatic delivery. This guard predates this change and is tested.

target.title: fix: allow fallback on prompt-stage harness-owned timeouts
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95574

When a harnessOwnsTransport provider (Ollama, OpenRouter) times out during a prompt, the failover policy returns `surface_error` immediately, bypassing the fallback chain entirely. This defeats the purpose of configured fallbacks for local model ? cloud fallback scenarios.

**Before:** Ollama timeout ? hard error "LLM request timed out", no fallback tried
**After:** Ollama timeout ? rotate profile ? fallback to next model in chain

## Evidence

- All 32 tests in `failover-policy.test.ts` pass (verified locally with vitest)
- The fix adds a single guard `!params.fallbackConfigured` to the prompt-stage timeout check
- When no fallback is configured, behavior is unchanged (surface_error)
- When fallback IS configured, the code falls through to the existing fallback check
- Diff: 2 files changed, 8 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

## Root Cause

In `resolveRunFailoverDecision`, the `harnessOwnsTransport && failoverReason === "timeout"` check returns `surface_error` before the `fallbackConfigured` check is reached. The fix adds `&& !params.fallbackConfigured` so the early return only fires when no fallback is configured.

target.title: feat(pairing): add configurable pairingMessage text per channel (#41058)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds optional `pairingMessage` config to iMessage and Telegram channel configs, allowing operators to customize or suppress OpenClaw branding in pairing challenge messages sent to unknown senders.

Fixes #41058

## Problem

When `dmPolicy: pairing` is set, every unknown sender receives a hardcoded message exposing:
1. The OpenClaw brand name
2. The channel platform ("Your iMessage sender id:" / "Your Telegram user id:")
3. The full CLI invocation syntax (`openclaw pairing approve imessage CODE`)

No config option exists to override any part of this. The only workaround is patching compiled dist bundles after every update — not sustainable for white-label or privacy-conscious deployments.

## What changed

- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfig` type with optional fields (`header`, `senderIdLabel`, `codeLabel`, `footer`, `showCliHint`). Update `buildPairingReply` to use config when provided.
- `src/config/types.imessage.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfigSchema`, wire into `IMessageAccountSchemaBase` and `TelegramAccountSchemaBase`
- `src/imessage/monitor/monitor-provider.ts`: Wire `pairingMessage` config into `issuePairingChallenge` via existing `buildReplyText` hook
- `src/telegram/dm-access.ts`: Pass `pairingMessage` through `enforceTelegramDmAccess`, wire into `issuePairingChallenge`
- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.test.ts`: Add tests for all config overrides and backward compat

## Fully backward compatible

All `PairingMessageConfig` fields are optional. Existing deployments with no `pairingMessage` config see zero behavior change.

## Example config

```yaml
channels:
  imessage:
    pairingMessage:
      header: 'Sender verification required.'
      senderIdLabel: 'Your sender ID:'
      footer: 'Share this code with your contact to complete verification.'
      showCliHint: false
```

## Notes

Discord, Signal, Slack, and LINE call sites follow the same pattern and can be extended in a follow-up if the team prefers to land iMessage/Telegram first.

## AI Disclosure
This PR was authored with AI assistance (OpenClaw/Jordan + Neo agents).
Testing: Fully tested — unit tests in `pairing-messages.test.ts` cover all config branches including edge cases; CI passes (types, lint, full test suite on Linux/Windows/macOS).
The authors have reviewed and understand all code changes in this PR.

commands.review_summary:
The PR caps explicit cron cloud stream idle waits at 60s and adds focused helper tests for cron cloud, short cron, and loopback local-provider cron cases.

PR surface: Source +7, Tests +17. Total +24 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main gives explicit cron run timeouts the full idle watchdog, and the PR changes that behavior based on local-provider classification. The compatibility defect is source-reproducible from the helper’s documented hostname limitation; no live local-hostname provider was run.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] There is a narrow mechanical blocker: preserve the PR’s stated local/self-hosted timeout compatibility for hostname-based provider URLs and add focused coverage.

target.title: fix: cron stream stalls fail over before job timeout
target.body:
Closes #85900

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where cron jobs with a pinned cloud model and configured fallbacks could wait until the outer cron timeout when the provider opened a stream but never sent chunks, preventing the existing fallback chain from trying the next model.

## Why This Change Was Made

Cron-triggered cloud model calls now cap explicit per-run stream idle waits at 60s so a stalled candidate fails early enough for `runWithModelFallback` to advance. Local/private providers keep their previous explicit cron timeout behavior because they can legitimately stay silent during long prompt evaluation, and explicit provider request timeouts continue to win for intentionally slow model servers.

## User Impact

Scheduled jobs with healthy fallback models recover from cloud stream stalls sooner instead of losing the whole cron run to the outer watchdog. Existing slow local/self-hosted cron jobs that set a long `timeoutSeconds` keep that longer idle window.

## Evidence

- `pnpm docs:list` to identify the relevant cron docs surface; read `docs/automation/cron-jobs.md`.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts` — pass.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts` — pass, 79 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` — pass, 82 tests.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --parallel-tests "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts"` — clean; parallel tests passed, 161 tests.
- `git diff --check` — pass.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Cron cloud model calls with explicit long `timeoutSeconds` no longer let a no-chunk stream stall consume the full cron watchdog before the existing model fallback chain can run.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/85900-cron-stream-stall-fallback` at `0c5868c58b2bf3720a8df88bd6fff3470f26825b`, macOS host, Node/pnpm workspace dependencies installed by `pnpm docs:list`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs } from "./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.ts"; const cases = { cronCloud300s: resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs({ trigger: "cron", runTimeoutMs: 300000 }), cronCloud30s: resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs({ trigger: "cron", runTimeoutMs: 30000 }), cronLocal600s: resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs({ trigger: "cron", runTimeoutMs: 600000, model: { baseUrl: "http://127.0.0.1:11434" } }), normalCloud300s: resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs({ runTimeoutMs: 300000 }) }; console.log(JSON.stringify(cases, null, 2));'
```

Focused tests also show the fallback loop continues to the next candidate after a provider-side error: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` passed with the existing unknown-error fallback coverage.

**Observed result after fix:** A cron cloud call with a 300s per-run timeout now uses a 60s stream idle watchdog, leaving the outer cron budget available for configured fallback candidates. A shorter 30s cron timeout still stays 30s, local/private provider cron calls keep their 600s explicit timeout, and normal non-cron cloud runs remain at the existing 120s default.

**What was not tested:** No live provider was forced into a real opened-but-silent network stream; the proof covers the source-level timeout selection and the existing model fallback retry behavior with focused Vitest coverage.

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB):**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, and when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed. The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, after merging current `openclaw/main` and cleaning unrelated formatter-only merge churn, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the private local-only QMD cache subpath, real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage, and local `qmd 2.1.0` on PATH. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used.
- **Exact steps run after this patch:** `node --import tsx --input-type=module` from the topic worktree with an inline driver. The driver created one session JSONL, ran `QmdMemoryManager.sync({ reason: "manual" })` across fresh manager instances, inspected and seeded `qmd_session_export_cache` through the private QMD helper subpath, rewrote the transcript with a deliberately stale fingerprint row whose size/mtime/inode matched the source, deleted the generated markdown target, and verified the next sync rebuilt it.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run:

```text
current head: eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30
qmd version: qmd 2.1.0
=== Phase 1: first sync writes markdown and SQLite cache ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=899
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=377
sync 1: sync done in 380 ms
cache target is export file: true
fingerprint length: 40
=== Phase 2: unchanged sync uses fast path ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=828
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=299
sync 2: sync done in 301 ms
export mtime unchanged: true
export size unchanged: true
=== Phase 3: matching stat row with stale content fingerprint rebuilds ===
seeded cache size matches source: true
seeded cache mtime matches source: true
seeded cache ino matches source: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=813
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=306
sync 3: sync done in 308 ms
export mtime newer: true
cache fingerprint changed: true
cache fingerprint length after rebuild: 40
=== Phase 4: delete markdown while SQLite cache survives ===
export exists after delete: false
sqlite cache row still present: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=818
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=307
sync 4: sync done in 309 ms
rebuilt export exists: true
cache target restored to export file: true

All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The first sync wrote markdown and a SQLite cache row with the expected derived target and 40-character fingerprint. The second sync preserved markdown mtime/size, proving unchanged input took the persisted fast path. The third sync rebuilt after a same-size rewrite even though the seeded cache row matched the source stat fields, proving the full-content fingerprint prevents stale skips. After deleting markdown while leaving SQLite cache intact, the fourth sync regenerated the target and restored the cache target to the expected export file.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, and batch-read follow-up. The proof above validates the current SQLite cache behavior and regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context.

## Verification

Current head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` includes:

- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts
# 56 passed

node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs
# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check
# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

pnpm tsgo:core
# exited 0

pnpm tsgo:core:test
# exited 0

git diff --check
# clean

node --import tsx --input-type=module
# real-runtime QMD/SQLite proof above; All runtime assertions passed.
```

target.title: fix(memory): prove live manager recovery after CLI reindex
target.body:
Closes #91167
Related #90453
Related #92943

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes the proof gap for #91167 where a running gateway/default memory manager could be suspected of staying stuck on `index metadata is missing` after a separate CLI/status reindex rebuilt the same SQLite store.

## Why This Change Was Made

Current `main` already publishes full memory reindexes into the canonical SQLite database instead of swapping the whole DB file, and the live manager refreshes index identity from that handle on status/search reads. This PR keeps the source change intentionally small by adding the missing regression coverage for that manager-owned live/CLI publication contract, without duplicating the broader in-flight reindex barrier work in #90453.

## User Impact

Users and maintainers get a pinned regression proving that the live gateway manager can recover visibility after an external CLI reindex without requiring a gateway restart for this #91167 path.

## Evidence

Real behavior proof:

- Behavior addressed: A live default memory manager with a missing index identity observes a separate `purpose: "cli"` forced reindex on the same SQLite store, refreshes to a valid index identity, and returns the newly indexed memory content without reopening the live manager.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout at `541917a3fd90`, Node `v24.15.0`, macOS host, real `node:sqlite` manager path with temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, built-in memory backend, FTS-only provider mode (`provider: "none"`) so no external credentials are required.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
RUN  v4.1.8 /Users/psx849261680/Desktop/projects/openclaw-91167

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)
Duration  35.12s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 93.46s

git diff --check: clean
oxfmt --check: All matched files use the correct format.
oxlint: clean
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
```

- Observed result after fix: The new regression deletes `memory_index_meta_v1` from an already-open live manager, runs a separate CLI-purpose manager `sync({ reason: "cli", force: true })` after changing the memory note, then verifies the original live manager reports `indexIdentity.status === "valid"` and `search("beta repaired")` returns the newly indexed `Beta topic` snippet.
- What was not tested: full Gateway process with a real tool invocation, remote embedding provider, large-index latency, and concurrent stress during an in-flight reindex; #90453 owns the in-flight reindex race/barrier proof.

Regression coverage added:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts` now covers the live-manager plus separate CLI-manager recovery path for #91167.

target.title: [codex] Confirm Telegram final delivery before completion
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Telegram final replies can currently be treated as completed when OpenClaw has only queued or attempted the final reply, rather than evidence that Telegram accepted and delivered a visible message. That matters because a generated final answer can disappear from the user conversation while the Codex/OpenClaw turn still finalizes as successful, leaving no retryable failure and no useful fallback.

This PR tightens that boundary for Telegram without changing silent/no-reply turns or non-Telegram legacy dispatcher behavior. It tracks explicit delivered counts, requires confirmed Telegram final delivery for routed and dispatcher final replies, and emits either a retryable failure or a clear fallback when a visible final was attempted but Telegram did not confirm delivery.

## Summary

- Track explicit delivery evidence in the shared reply dispatcher via delivered counts.
- Require confirmed Telegram final delivery before treating dispatcher/routed final replies as completed, while preserving legacy dispatcher behavior when delivered counts are unavailable.
- Make Telegram emit a retryable failure or fallback when a visible final was attempted but Telegram did not confirm delivery, without breaking silent/no-reply or reasoning-only suppression paths.

## Evidence

- Reproduced the real failure class locally from Telegram delivery history: a final reply was generated but Telegram delivery failed, while the surrounding turn still risked looking complete from the agent side.
- Added focused dispatcher coverage for explicit delivery evidence: `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.ts` now records delivered counts only when the delivery callback returns usable delivery evidence.
- Added routed/final outcome coverage in `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.final-outcome-counts.test.ts` and `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-flow.test.ts`.
- Added Telegram behavior coverage in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` for unconfirmed final delivery fallback and retryable failure when fallback is suppressed.
- Local validation passed:
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/reply-flow.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.final-outcome-counts.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.reply-dispatch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.final-outcome-counts.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-flow.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `git diff --check`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
  - `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`

## Notes

- Upstream main already contains the Telegram local/relative Markdown link fix, so this PR intentionally targets the remaining final-delivery confirmation gap.
- I also ran `node scripts/test-projects.mjs --changed origin/main`. It passed the large unit-fast shards, then failed in untouched `src/security/install-policy.test.ts` with `ENOENT` reading a temporary `forked.pid`; rerunning that test alone reproduced the same pre-existing failure.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, the Telegram bot exhibited text "churn" — users would briefly see a text message appear, then it would be deleted and replaced by a voice note with the same text as caption. This happened on **both** the ACP dispatch path and the non-ACP dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`).

This PR eliminates the churn on **both paths** by introducing a `captionedFinalText` channel capability, suppressing live block delivery and partial streaming when captioned final TTS will be used, and correctly handling tagged auto-TTS mode with a split eligibility boolean.

## Root cause

```
Before (main):
  1. Block text / partial streaming delivers text to user immediately
  2. TTS synthesis runs (2-5 seconds)
  3. Voice note + caption delivered as separate message
  Result: user sees text first, then voice note = churn (#83511)

After (this PR):
  1. willUseCaptionedFinalTts = true (channel supports captioned voice + final TTS mode)
  2. Partial streaming suppressed, block text delivery suppressed
  3. TTS synthesis runs, text accumulated silently
  4. Voice note + caption delivered as single message (or text fallback if TTS fails)
  Result: user sees only the voice note with caption = no churn
```

### Tagged auto-TTS mode

When `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"`, TTS only fires for replies containing `[[tts:...]]` directives. Three additional issues were discovered and fixed:

1. **Split boolean**: `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` was doing two jobs — gating live text suppression AND caption eligibility. Split into `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` (live text suppression, excludes tagged) and `canCaptionFinalTts` (caption eligibility, includes tagged).

2. **Block TTS gate**: The accumulated block TTS fallback was gated on `replies.length === 0`, but Anthropic streaming returns both block callbacks and a final reply payload. Changed to gate on `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` and `queuedFinal` instead.

3. **Partial streaming leak**: `onPartialReply` was active for tagged mode, sending raw `[[tts:text]]...[[/tts:text]]` directive text to Telegram during streaming, creating a duplicate text message before the voice+caption arrived. Fixed by gating `onPartialReply` with `canCaptionFinalTts` (covers both always and tagged modes) instead of `willUseCaptionedFinalTts`.

### Media block preservation

When captioned final TTS is active, block suppression is narrowed to **text-only** payloads. Blocks containing media (`mediaUrl`, `mediaUrls`), `presentation`, `interactive`, or `channelData` are delivered immediately with their text stripped (to avoid duplicating the caption). This applies to both ACP and non-ACP dispatch paths.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` + `canCaptionFinalTts`: suppress partial streaming and block text for both always and tagged modes; narrow block suppression to text-only payloads (media blocks delivered with text stripped); `sendFinalPayload` returns `deliveredMedia`; block TTS gate changed from `replies.length === 0` to `!deliveredFinalTtsMedia && !queuedFinal` |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` | Regression tests: tagged directive-only voice+caption, tagged+always both-block-and-final delivery, partial suppression for tagged TTS on caption-capable channels, plain tagged block delivery, media block delivery with text stripped, text-only block suppression |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` gate: suppress ACP block delivery and defer text settlement; `canCaptionFinalTts` for tagged mode; `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` tracking |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` | `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` state tracking + `hasDeliveredFinalTtsMedia()` accessor; narrow `suppressBlockUserDelivery` to text-only blocks (media blocks delivered with text stripped) |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts` | Regression tests: media block delivery with text stripped under `suppressBlockUserDelivery`, text-only block suppression |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts` | Regression tests: deferred settlement, block suppression, tagged streaming, tagged directive TTS, ACP live text deferral |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts` | `deferLiveTextUntilFinalFlush` option to suppress live text flushing for tagged TTS |
| `src/tts/directives.ts` | `resolveDirectiveOnlyTtsCaptionText` parser helper to extract clean caption from directive-only responses |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.ts` | `captionedFinalText: true` capability declaration |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts` | Test for capability declaration |
| `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` | JSDoc for `captionedFinalText` capability contract |
| `src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts` | Capability seam test |
| `docs/tools/tts.md` | Document `captionedFinalText` capability: opt-in criteria via `capabilities.tts.voice`, tagged mode behavior, text-only fallback, plugin API snippet |
| `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md` | Document `captionedFinalText` capability in channel plugin SDK guide: opt-in declaration, media block preservation, fallback behavior, tagged mode |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updated SDK API baseline hash |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
 ✓ 202 passed (202)

$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts
 ✓ 29 passed (29)

$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ 50 passed (50)

$ npx vitest run extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts
 ✓ 11 passed (11)

$ npx vitest run src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts
 ✓ 1 passed (1)

Total: 293 tests passed across 5 test suites
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram TTS final-mode text-before-voice churn on both ACP and non-ACP dispatch paths, plus tagged mode partial preview leaking raw `[[tts:text]]` directives, plus media block suppression during captioned final TTS.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at commit `05b4483a8f`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Live Telegram bot (`@thomas_fu_bot`) with Microsoft TTS (`zh-CN-XiaoxiaoNeural`), `messages.tts.mode: "final"`.

- **Failing proof before fix (live Telegram)**:

  ![Before fix — separate text message + voice message (churn)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-before-fix.png)

  User sent "hello" to the Telegram bot. The bot responded with **two separate messages**: a text message AND a voice note — instead of a single voice note with caption.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Built and installed from current head: `npm run build && npm pack && npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz`
  2. Registered and restarted Gateway: `openclaw gateway install && openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Tested with `messages.tts.auto: "always"` and `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"` — sent plain messages and directive-bearing messages to `@thomas_fu_bot` on Telegram

- **Evidence after fix**: Always mode (`messages.tts.auto: "always"`) at commit `05b4483a8f`:

  ![Always mode — voice with caption, single message](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-always-mode.png)

  Always-mode replies produce a single voice note with caption text. No text churn, no separate text bubble.

- **Evidence — Media block preservation with captioned final TTS at commit `05b4483a8f`**:

  ![Media block preserved — image delivered + voice note with caption](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-media-block.png)

  When the bot generates an image during a TTS-enabled conversation (`auto: "always"`), the image is delivered immediately while text is deferred to the voice note caption. No media blocks are suppressed, and caption text does not duplicate as a separate text message.

- **Observed result after fix**: Always TTS auto mode delivers voice notes as single messages with caption text on Telegram. Media blocks (images, files) are preserved and delivered immediately with text stripped. No text flash, no duplicate messages, no media loss.

- **What was not tested**: Other TTS providers (only Microsoft TTS tested live). Group chat TTS (tested in DM only). Other channels with `captionedFinalText` capability (only Telegram tested). Tagged mode live proof pending — covered by unit tests (partial suppression + directive-only caption regression tests added).

Closes #83511
